items.question: Define deep learning and name its two defining characteristics in this roadmap.
items.answer: Deep learning is ML using multi-layer neural networks to learn representations end-to-end from data. The two defining characteristics are (1) neural networks with multiple stacked layers and (2) representation learning instead of primarily hand-crafted features.

items.question: How does deep learning relate to machine learning and generative AI?
items.answer: DL is a subset of ML focused on deep neural nets. Generative AI is largely built on DL methods but targets creation of new content; DL also covers discriminative tasks like classification and detection.

items.question: When would you recommend classical ML instead of deep learning?
items.answer: When data are small or tabular, features are well understood, interpretability and fast iteration matter, or compute and labeling budgets are tight — trees, linear models, or boosting often win before investing in deep representation learning.

items.question: What is representation learning and why does depth help?
items.answer: Representation learning means hidden layers discover task-relevant features during training. Depth stacks transformations so early layers capture simple patterns and later layers compose them into complex concepts aligned with the task objective.

items.question: Give an example application where deep learning is a natural fit.
items.answer: Image classification from raw pixels: convolutional layers learn edge and texture filters, deeper layers combine them into object detectors, trained end-to-end on large labeled image sets — difficult to replicate with manual features alone.
